meta.title: L405 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Land Rover Range Rover L405 engine — 3.0 TDV6/SDV6, 4.4 SDV8, 5.0 V8 SC. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and a clear 60% scrap threshold. Get free quotes from 12 vetted Land Rover specialists today.

@graph.name: Land Rover Range Rover L405 Engine Codes
Land Rover Range Rover L405 Core Variants

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover L405 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned L405 engine typically costs £4,500–£14,000 supply only [EM-QUOTE], depending on engine — the 3.0 TDV6 sits at £4,500–£7,000, the 4.4 SDV8 at £6,500–£10,500, and the 5.0 V8 at £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £900–£1,600. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Land Rover Range Rover L405 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.4 SDV8 (448DT) is widely considered the most reliable L405 engine — timing chains and tensioners are usually faultless, and there is no catastrophic crank failure risk. The 3.0 V6 Supercharged petrol is also a solid, overlooked option that avoids the diesel's crank issues.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover L405?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common issues include: crankshaft bearing failure on the 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 diesel; timing chain stretch and coolant crossover pipe failure on the 5.0 V8 Supercharged; oil pump failure on the 4.4 SDV8; and air suspension compressor and strut leaks across all variants.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover L405 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine. The 4.4 SDV8 is robust and trouble‑free when maintained. The 3.0 TDV6 and SDV6 carry the highest risk — crank failure can be catastrophic. The 5.0 V8 has well‑documented timing chain issues. The L405's aluminium chassis is corrosion‑resistant, but the air suspension is a known weak point.

mainEntity.name: Which L405 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don't recommend outright avoidance, but a 3.0 TDV6 or SDV6 without documented engine history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The crank bearing failure risk is well documented, and a replacement can cost £7,000+ — a significant portion of the car's value.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my L405 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: 3.0 diesel — rumbling/knocking sound, especially under load; 5.0 V8 — timing chain rattle on cold start, coolant loss with no external leak; 4.4 SDV8 — oil pressure warning, turbo failure symptoms. Use our diagnostic guide to identify the specific symptoms.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Range Rover L405 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply our 60% Land Rover threshold. A £7,000 SDV6 recon against a £15,000 L405 is 47% — viable. Against a £10,000 example, the same recon is 70% — not worth it. For a 5.0 V8, a £12,000 recon on a £20,000 car is 60% — borderline. Value the car first; if the repair exceeds 60%, walk away.

mainEntity.name: How long do Range Rover L405 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.4 SDV8 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6's limiting factor is the crank bearing, which often fails between 60,000–120,000 miles — addressed proactively, it can last longer. The 5.0 V8's timing chain can stretch at 60,000–100,000 miles; addressed proactively, the engine is durable.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the L405 and L460?

acceptedAnswer.text: The L405 is the fourth‑generation Range Rover, produced from 2012 to 2021. The L460 is the fifth‑generation Range Rover, launched in 2022, with a completely new platform, new BMW‑derived engines, and updated hybrid technology. The L460 replaces the L405 and is covered on its own Generation Page.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Range Rover L405 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping varies by engine. For the 3.0 diesel, the code is stamped on the block near the starter motor. For the 4.4 SDV8 and 5.0 V8, it's on the block face below the inlet manifold. Tell us your registration and we'll identify it for free.

hero.tagPill: Land Rover Range Rover L405 • SUV • 2012–2021
hero.h1: Land Rover Range Rover L405 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the fourth-generation Range Rover — from the 3.0 TDV6 diesel to the 5.0-litre supercharged V8. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the crankshaft and timing chain risks that define this generation's ownership story. Backed by 284+ real UK L405 enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Land Rover Range Rover L405 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the L405 Range Rover — from the 2012 launch to the 2021 phase-out, including the 2018 facelift updates.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any L405 engine family. The 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 ratings reflect the well-documented crankshaft bearing failure risk. The 4.4 SDV8 is rated higher based on specialist consensus that it is "very reliable" and "brilliant", with timing chains "usually faultless, unlike the 5.0 petrols". The 5.0 V8 rating reflects timing chain stretch at low mileage and coolant crossover pipe failures.

overview.h2: Land Rover Range Rover L405 — Overview
overview.intro: The L405 is the fourth generation of the full-size Range Rover, launched in 2012 and produced until 2021. It was the first SUV to feature an all-aluminium monocoque chassis, shedding up to 420kg compared to its predecessor, the L322. The engine lineup spanned the 3.0-litre TDV6 and SDV6 diesels, the 4.4-litre SDV8 diesel, the 3.0-litre supercharged V6 petrol, and the 5.0-litre supercharged V8 petrol, plus a diesel hybrid from 2015 and a plug-in hybrid (P400e) from 2018. A 2018 facelift brought revised styling, updated infotainment, and the P400e hybrid.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: JLR D7u aluminium monocoque • Predecessor: L322 • Successor: L460 • Sibling body styles in this generation: none — L405 is the full-size Range Rover; Range Rover Sport L494 is a separate model.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The L405 generated 284 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], with strong demand for both the 4.4 SDV8 and the 3.0 diesel variants.

items.quote: The SDV8 is a brilliant, brilliant combination — very reliable, relentless torque, and low 30s mpg possible. Timing chains and tensioners are usually faultless. Oil dilution is a known issue but not universal.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want flagship diesel refinement without the TDV6's crank risk.

items.quote: Rare, but they won't have the same issues as the SDV6 or the 5.0 SC. Smooth power delivery and genuinely the cleanest option among the V6 and V8 petrols.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement without the 5.0's timing chain worries.

items.quote: Crankshaft and bearing failures are the defining risk of this generation. The narrow bearing design causes crankshaft fatigue and oil starvation. A snapped crank means a full long engine replacement.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who are prepared for a potential £7,000+ engine replacement.

items.quote: Timing chain stretch at low mileage is the most famous problem. Coolant crossover pipes under the supercharger are hard to see and can fail catastrophically. The supercharger coupler can also fail.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who prioritise V8 theatre over running costs.

items.quote: The SDV8 costs more upfront but delivers the best combination of reliability, torque, and refinement. No catastrophic crank failure risk, no timing chain woes. The ownership costs are higher, but the risk profile is dramatically lower.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best L405 engine and are willing to pay for it.

items.quote: Rarely discussed, but the supercharged V6 petrol avoids the crank failure of the diesels and the timing chain issues of the 5.0 V8. It's smooth, refined, and genuinely the quietest option in the range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement without the V8's complexity.

ownershipEconomics.h2: Land Rover Range Rover L405 — Ownership Economics

rows.ownershipVerdict: Highest Risk — crank failure is catastrophic; avoid unless proven history.
Highest Risk — same crank issue as TDV6; twin-turbo adds complexity.
Best Diesel — reliable, torquey, but expensive to replace.
Solid Choice — rare but reliable; fewer horror stories.
Most Expensive — theatre comes at a cost; budget for chain work.
Future-Proof — complex but post-2018 units are well-sorted.

economicsRule.title: The L405 rule
economicsRule.text: A £7,000 SDV6 recon against a £20,000 L405 sits at 35% — well within our 60% Land Rover threshold. Against a £12,000 example, the same recon is 58% — close to the line. The 5.0 V8 is another matter: a £12,000 recon against a £20,000 car is 60% — on the line. Value the car first, then the repair. And always, always check the crank history on any 3.0 diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L405 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 4.4 SDV8 — higher upfront but no catastrophic failure risk.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L405 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: 5.0 V8 Supercharged — timing chain work and fuel bills add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which L405 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: 4.4 SDV8 — flagship diesel refinement without the TDV6's crank risk.

problems.title: 3.0 TDV6/SDV6 Crankshaft Bearing Failure
problems.rootCause: The 3.0-litre diesel retains the same narrow bearing design as the older 2.7 TDV6, which can cause crankshaft fatigue and oil starvation. The engine makes a loud rumbling/knocking sound when running, which worsens under load. When the crankshaft snaps or bearings spin, the only reliable fix is a full long engine replacement.

tieredCosts.work: Oil analysis; bearing inspection; sump drop
Full reconditioned short or long engine

problems.recommendation: If your L405 3.0 diesel develops a rumbling or knocking sound, stop driving immediately and have the oil analysed. A £800–£2,000 preventative inspection is far cheaper than the £6,000–£9,500 replacement that follows a snapped crank.

cta.label: Compare 3.0 TDV6 engine prices for your L405 →

problems.title: 5.0 V8 Supercharged Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The most famous engine problem is the stretching of the timing chains at low mileage. The supercharger drive can also hum and require replacement. Coolant crossover pipes under the supercharger are hard to see and can fail catastrophically.

tieredCosts.work: Timing chain, tensioner and guide replacement; coolant pipe upgrade
tieredCosts.note: Yes — cheap insurance against catastrophic failure

problems.recommendation: On any L405 5.0 V8 over 60,000 miles without documented chain history, budget for a timing chain inspection and replacement. A £1,500–£3,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £8,000–£14,000 replacement that follows a chain failure.

cta.label: Compare 5.0 V8 engine prices for your L405 →

problems.title: 4.4 SDV8 Oil Pump and Turbo Failure
problems.rootCause: Oil pump seizure is a known issue that can lead to secondary turbo failure. Other than leaking oil and coolant, these are pretty solid engines. Timing chains and tensioners are usually faultless.

tieredCosts.work: Oil pump inspection; turbo oil feed pipe replacement

problems.recommendation: On any 4.4 SDV8 approaching 75,000 miles, have the oil pump inspected and the turbo oil feed pipes checked. A £600–£1,500 preventative service is far cheaper than the £6,500–£10,500 replacement that follows a seized oil pump.

cta.label: Compare 4.4 SDV8 engine prices for your L405 →

problems.title: Air Suspension System Failures
problems.affectedModels: All L405 variants, particularly higher-mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry on this generation. The air suspension compressor can fail, and air struts can leak, leading to a "suspension fault" warning and a car that sits unevenly.
problems.recommendation: Have the air suspension inspected at every service — a £400–£900 compressor rebuild is far cheaper than a £1,500+ full system replacement.

cta.label: Compare L405 air suspension repair quotes →

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are Range Rover L405 only. The Range Rover Sport L494 and the later L460 are separate models with different engine availability and are covered on their own pages.
